verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: On the Soul and the Resurrection.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The mind, in times of bereavement, craves a certainty gained by reasoning as to the existence of the soul after death.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: First, then: Virtue will be impossible, if deprived of the life of eternity, her only advantage.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But this is a moral argument.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The case calls for speculative and scientific treatment.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: How is the objection that the nature of the soul, as of real things, is material, to be met?

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Thus; the truth of this doctrine would involve the truth of Atheism; whereas Atheism is refuted by the fact of the wise order that reigns in the world.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: In other words, the spirituality of God cannot be denied: and this proves the possibility of spiritual or immaterial existence: and therefore, that of the soul.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But is God, then, the same thing as the soul?

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: No: but man is “a little world in himself;” and we may with the same right conclude from this Microcosm to the actual existence of an immaterial soul, as from the phenomena of the world to the reality of God’s existence.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: A Definition of the soul is then given, for the sake of clearness in the succeeding discussion.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: It is a created, living, intellectual being, with the power, as long as it is provided with organs, of sensuous perception.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: For “the mind sees,” not the eye; take, for instance, the meaning of the phases of the moon.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The objection that the “organic machine” of the body produces all thought is met by the instance of the water-organ.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Such machines, if thought were really an attribute of matter, ought to build themselves spontaneously: whereas they are a direct proof of an invisible thinking power in man.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: A work of Art means mind: there is a thing perceived, and a thing not perceived.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But still, what is this thing not perceived?

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: If it has no sensible quality whatever—Where is it?

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The answer is, that the same question might be asked about the Deity (Whose existence is not denied).

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Then the Mind and the Deity are identical?

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Not so: in its substantial existence, as separable from matter, the soul is like God; but this likeness does not extend to sameness; it resembles God as a copy the original.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: As being “simple and uncompounded” the soul survives the dissolution of the composite body, whose scattered elements it will continue to accompany, as if watching over its property till the Resurrection, when it will clothe itself in them anew.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The soul was defined “an intellectual being.”

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But anger and desire are not of the body either.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Are there, then, two or three souls?—Answer.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Anger and desire do not belong to the essence of the soul, but are only among its varying states; they are not originally part of ourselves, and we can and must rid ourselves of them, and bring them, as long as they continue to mark our community with the brute creation, into the service of the good.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: They are the “tares” of the heart, while they serve any other purpose.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But where will the soul “accompany its elements”?—Hades is not a particular spot; it means the Invisible; those passages in the Bible in which the regions under the earth are alluded to are explained as allegorical, although the partizans of the opposite interpretation need not be combated.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But how will the soul know the scattered elements of the once familiar form?

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: This is answered by two illustrations (not analogies).

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The skill of the painter, the force that has united numerous colours to form a single tint, will, if (by some miracle) that actual tint was to fall back into those various colours, be cognizant of each one of these last, e.g. the tone and size of the drop of gold, of red, &c.; and could at will recombine them.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The owner of a cup of clay would know its fragments (by their shape) amidst a mass of fragments of clay vessels of other shapes, or even if they were plunged again into their native clay.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: So the soul knows its elements amidst their “kindred dust”; or when each one has flitted back to its own primeval source on the confines of the Universe.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But how does this harmonize with the Parable of the Rich Man and Lazarus?

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The bodies of both were in the grave: and so all that is said of them is in a spiritual sense.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But the soul can suffer still, being cognizant, not only of the elements of the whole body, but of those that formed each member, e.g. the tongue.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: By the relations of the Rich Man are meant the impressions made on his soul by the things of flesh and blood.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But if we must have no emotions in the next world, how shall there be virtue, and how shall there be love of God?

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: For anger, we saw, contributed to the one, desire to the other.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: We shall be like God so far that we shall always contemplate the Beautiful in Him.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Now, God, in contemplating Himself, has no desire and hope, no regret and memory.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The moment of fruition is always present, and so His Love is perfect, without the need of any emotion.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: So will it be with us.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: God draws “that which belongs to Him” to this blessed passionlessness; and in this very drawing consists the torment of a passion-laden soul.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Severe and long-continued pains in eternity are thus decreed to sinners, not because God hates them, nor for the sake alone of punishing them; but “because what belongs to God must at any cost be preserved for Him.”

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The degree of pain which must be endured by each one is necessarily proportioned to the measure of the wickedness.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: God will thus be “all in all”; yet the loved one’s form will then be woven, though into a more ethereal texture, of the same elements as before.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: (This is not Nirvana.)

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Here the doctrine of the Resurrection is touched.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The Christian Resurrection and that of the heathen philosophies coincide in that the soul is reclothed from some elements of the Universe.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But there are fatal objections to the latter under its two forms:

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Transmigration pure and simple;

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The Platonic Soul-rotation.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Obliterates the distinction between the mineral or vegetable, and the spiritual, world.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Makes it a sin to eat and drink.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Confuse the moral choice.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Make heaven the cradle of vice, and earth of virtue.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Contradict the truth that they assume, that there is no change in heaven.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Attribute every birth to a vice, and therefore are either Atheist or Manichæan.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Make a life a chapter of accidents.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Contradict facts of moral character.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: God is the cause of our life, both in body and soul.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But when and how does the soul come into existence?

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The how we can never know.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: There are objections to seeking the material for any created thing either in God, or outside God.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But we may regard the whole Creation as the realized thoughts of God.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: (Anticipation of Malebranche.)

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The when may be determined.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Objections to the existence of soul before body have been given above.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: But soul is necessary to life, and the embryo lives.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Therefore soul is not born after body.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: So body and soul are born together.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: As to the number of souls, Humanity itself is a thought of God not yet completed, as these continual additions prove.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: When it is completed, this “progress of Humanity” will cease, by there being no more births: and no births, no deaths.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Before answering objections to the Scriptural doctrine of the Resurrection, the passages that contain it are mentioned: especially Psalm cxviii.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: The various objections to it, to the Purgatory to follow, and to the Judgment, are then stated; especially that

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: A man is not the same being (physically) two days together.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: Which phase of him, then, is to rise again, be tortured (if need be), and judged?

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: They are all answered by a Definition of the Resurrection, i.e. the restoration of man to his original state.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: In that, there is neither age nor infancy; and the “coats of skins” are laid aside.

verses.book: On the Soul and the Resurrection.
verses.text: When the process of purification has been completed, the better attributes of the soul appear—imperishability, life, honour, grace, glory, power, and, in short, all that belongs to human nature as the image of Deity.
